name: Fetal glucose utilisation per kg
tier_rationale: Tier B from Battaglia & Meschia 1986 textbook 'An Introduction to Fetal Physiology': the canonical reference for fetal substrate utilisation rates derived from chronically-catheterised lamb studies. Human extrapolation by body-mass scaling.
notes: Net glucose utilisation by the late-gestation human fetus per kilogram body weight.

name: Fetal oxygen consumption per kg
tier_rationale: Tier B from Battaglia & Meschia 1986 textbook: fetal oxygen consumption ~7 mL/kg/min from lamb studies, well-replicated in subsequent ovine work; human estimate by body-mass scaling.
notes: Whole-body oxygen consumption of the late-gestation human fetus per kilogram body weight.

name: Fetal haemoglobin (HbF) P50 at term
tier_rationale: Tier A from Bauer, Ludwig & Ludwig 1969 Respiration Physiology (PMID 4980905, DOI 10.1016/0034-5687(69)90010-3), the foundational study comparing adult vs fetal haemoglobin O2 affinity. Fetal HbF P50 ~19-20 mmHg is much lower than adult HbA (~26.6), reflecting HbF's reduced affinity for 2,3-DPG. The left-shift is the key adaptation enabling fetal blood to extract O2 from low-PO2 placental blood. Triangulated across every standard hematology and neonatal-physiology reference.

extraction.method: triangulated from Bauer 1969 + standard hematology references

notes: The 7 mmHg leftward P50 shift relative to adult HbA is the key biophysical adaptation enabling fetal-side O2 extraction.

name: Fetal haemoglobin concentration at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from standard neonatal-haematology references: cord-blood haemoglobin at term ~17 g/dL — significantly higher than maternal (~11.5 g/dL). The high Hb concentration partially compensates for the low umbilical-vein PO2: fetal arterial O2 content is comparable to adult despite much lower PO2. Hytten & Chamberlain 1980 covers fetal haematology; modern neonatal guidelines confirm.
notes: Higher fetal Hb + leftward P50 together close most of the gap between fetal-side PO2 and adult-side PO2 in arterial O2 content.

name: Umbilical artery pH at term (cord blood)
tier_rationale: Tier B from Battaglia & Meschia 1986 and standard neonatal practice: term umbilical-artery pH (deoxygenated fetal blood returning to the placenta) is typically 7.20-7.34 with mean ~7.27. Values <7.10 trigger clinical concern; <7.00 with elevated base deficit defines significant acidaemia. The arterial-venous pH gradient (~0.07) reflects net acid load from fetal tissue metabolism.

applicability.population: Healthy term newborn, cord blood, uncomplicated vaginal delivery

notes: Cord-blood pH is the standard clinical metric for intrapartum fetal acid-base status.

name: Umbilical vein pH at term (cord blood)
tier_rationale: Tier B from Battaglia & Meschia 1986: term umbilical-vein pH (freshly oxygenated blood returning from the placenta to the fetus) is typically 7.28-7.40 with mean ~7.34. The vein-artery pH difference (~0.07) is the principal indicator that the cord-blood sample was correctly identified — a small or absent gradient suggests the sample was drawn from the wrong vessel.

applicability.population: Healthy term newborn, cord blood, uncomplicated vaginal delivery

name: Umbilical artery lactate at term (cord blood)
tier_rationale: Tier C from Battaglia & Meschia 1986 and standard obstetric texts: cord-blood lactate at uncomplicated term delivery is typically 2-6 mmol/L (well above adult resting ~1 mmol/L). The high baseline reflects partial reliance on anaerobic metabolism in the fetal circulation and the burst of activity at delivery. Lactate >8 mmol/L is associated with intrapartum hypoxia. Distribution is heavily right-skewed; central value ~3.5 reflects the modal cohort mean.

applicability.population: Healthy term newborn, cord blood, uncomplicated vaginal delivery

name: Fetal plasma glucose at term (cord blood)
tier_rationale: Tier B from Battaglia & Meschia 1986: fetal plasma glucose is closely coupled to maternal glucose by trans-placental GLUT1/GLUT3 transport and runs at approximately 70-75% of maternal levels. Term cord values typically 2.8-4.5 mmol/L. Fetal glucose follows maternal glucose dynamically; the fetus does not contribute substantial endogenous glucose production until the perinatal switch.

applicability.population: Healthy term newborn, cord blood, mother fasted ~overnight

notes: Fetal/maternal glucose ratio ~0.7 is one of the most robust quantitative facts in fetal metabolism.

name: Fetal pulmonary fluid net secretion rate, mid-gestation baseline
tier_rationale: Tier D (hypothesis-only): Strang 1991 reviews fetal lung liquid as chloride-driven secretion at ~4-5 mL/kg/h through most of gestation. Human in vivo rates are extrapolated from ovine and other animal data and are not directly measurable in human fetuses; the central value is illustrative. DO NOT USE FOR PREDICTION.
notes: Hypothesis-only Tier D parameter used in fetal_pulmonary_fluid_trajectory submodel. Positive sign = secretion (fluid into lung lumen).

name: Fetal pulmonary fluid net rate near term (transition to reabsorption)
tier_rationale: Tier D (hypothesis-only): near term, fetal lung liquid net flux reverses from secretion to active sodium-driven reabsorption (Strang 1991); during labour reabsorption accelerates further. Quantitative human rates are species-extrapolated and the timing of the switch varies. DO NOT USE FOR PREDICTION.
notes: Hypothesis-only Tier D parameter. Negative sign = net reabsorption.
